name: Futsu-no-Mitama
description: Futsu-no-Mitama, meaning "Spirit of Futsu," is a legendary sword in Japanese mythology, revered for its divine origins and immense power. Often described as a chokutō (straight sword), it is not merely a weapon but a sacred artifact embodying the kami (spirit or god) Futsu-nushi. Its creation and subsequent use are intertwined with the establishment of the Japanese nation and the subjugation of rebellious deities and earthly forces. The blade is said to possess an inherent spiritual potency capable of vanquishing evil and establishing order. Its association with powerful figures like Takemikazuchi-no-kami further solidifies its role as a symbol of divine authority and martial prowess. Legends attribute to it the power to quell unrest and ensure the prosperity of the land. Even a single strike from Futsu-no-Mitama could bring down enemies. The sword is more than a tool; it is an embodiment of divine will and a testament to the power of the gods in shaping the mortal realm.
subtitle: A divine sword of Japanese mythology.
origin: Created by the gods, specifically associated with Futsu-nushi and Takemikazuchi.
currentLocation: Various shrines across Japan; exact location debated, often kept in secrecy.

keyMyths.title: The Pacification of Japan
keyMyths.description: According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Takemikazuchi used Futsu-no-Mitama to subdue rebellious deities and unruly tribes in the process of pacifying Japan. The sword's power was instrumental in establishing the authority of the Yamato court and consolidating the nation under a unified rule. Its mere presence inspired awe and obedience.

keyMyths.title: Association with Futsu-nushi
keyMyths.description: Futsu-no-Mitama is strongly linked to the deity Futsu-nushi, who is often considered the spirit residing within the sword. This connection elevates the sword beyond a simple weapon, transforming it into a tangible manifestation of divine power and a conduit for Futsu-nushi's will in the mortal world, providing protection and strength to its wielder.
keyMyths.source: Various Shinto texts and oral traditions

keyMyths.title: Symbol of Divine Authority
keyMyths.description: Beyond its martial capabilities, Futsu-no-Mitama serves as a powerful symbol of divine authority and legitimacy. Its possession signifies a connection to the gods and the right to rule. This symbolic value underscores the sword's importance in legitimizing the power of those who wield it or claim descent from those who did, solidifying their position in the social and political hierarchy.
keyMyths.source: Historical accounts and interpretations of Shinto beliefs

sources.author: Philippi, Donald L.
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, providing detailed accounts of the creation myths and the role of deities like Takemikazuchi and Futsu-nushi.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, providing detailed accounts of the creation myths and the role of deities like Takemikazuchi and Futsu-nushi.

sources.author: Aston, W.G.
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: A comprehensive English translation of the Nihon Shoki, offering a different perspective on the same myths and historical events described in the Kojiki.
sources.text: A comprehensive English translation of the Nihon Shoki, offering a different perspective on the same myths and historical events described in the Kojiki.

sources.author: Reader, Ian
sources.description: Provides insights into the continued significance of Shinto beliefs and practices in modern Japan, including the reverence for sacred objects like Futsu-no-Mitama.
